text_blocks.text: The worship of dynastic ruler cults was also a
feature of this period, most notably in Egypt,
where the Ptolemies adopted earlier Pharaonic
practice, and established themselves as god-kings.
These cults were usually associated with a specific
temple in honor of the ruler such as the
Ptolemaieia at Alexandria and had their own
festivals and theatrical performances. The setting
up of ruler cults was more based on the
systematized honors offered to the kings (sacrifice,
proskynesis, statues, altars, hymns) which put
them on par with the gods (isotheism) than on
actual belief of their divine nature. According to
Peter Green, these cults did not produce genuine
belief of the divinity of rulers among the Greeks
and Macedonians. The worship of Alexander was
also popular, as in the long lived cult at Erythrae
and of course, at Alexandria, where his tomb was
